tata.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2023 is consolidated while FY2024 is standalone

sections.about: This metric measures other non-hazardous waste generated under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator on category-wise waste generation. In integrated steelmaking, this typically captures large-volume mineral by-products such as slag, dust, scale and other non-hazardous residues. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Other non-hazardous waste is the dominant driver of total waste for all large steelmakers. In FY2025, Jindal was lowest at 1,868,802 MT, SAIL reported 13,841,460 MT, Tata 16,119,942 MT and JSW 18,950,286 MT. Tata’s increase from 15,293,347 MT in FY2024 to 16,119,942 MT in FY2025 drove its total waste worsening, while JSL’s lower absolute number reflects smaller scale and stainless scrap/EAF route.
sections.tataPositioning: Tata ranked 3rd in FY2025 and generated about 763% more other non-hazardous waste than Jindal. Tata improved rank from 4th to 3rd only because JSW’s non-hazardous waste rose more sharply; Tata’s own volume worsened.
sections.targets: SAIL targets reducing solid waste generation and maximising utilisation to 100%. No Tata metric-specific non-hazardous-waste reduction target was identified in the retrieved extracts.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Absolute non-hazardous waste is strongly scale-driven; intensity metrics are more decision-useful for production-normalised comparison. Tata Steel FY2022-23 data was not reported on a purely standalone basis but rather collectively for Tata Steel India and its Indian subsidiaries.
